slides.heading: The Rise of Humanoid Robots
slides.body: From science fiction to factory floors — the machines that walk like us are finally here

slides.heading: Why Human-Shaped?
slides.body: Our world is built for human bodies — door handles, stairs, tools. A humanoid robot can operate in existing spaces without costly redesigns, making it far more versatile than purpose-built industrial arms.

slides.heading: The Hardware Breakthrough
slides.body: Advances in electric actuators, lightweight carbon-fiber frames, and compact battery packs have finally made human-sized robots practical. Modern humanoids can walk, run, and recover from stumbles in real time.

slides.heading: AI as the Brain
slides.body: Large language models and vision AI give robots the ability to understand spoken instructions, interpret their environment, and adapt to unexpected situations — a leap beyond hard-coded automation.

slides.heading: Who's Building Them?
slides.body: Tesla (Optimus), Figure AI, Boston Dynamics (Atlas), Agility Robotics (Digit), and dozens of startups are racing to ship commercial humanoids. China's UBTECH and Unitree are close behind, fueling a global competition.

slides.heading: First Jobs on the List
slides.body: Early deployments focus on repetitive, physically demanding tasks: moving boxes in warehouses, assembling car parts, and sorting packages. BMW and Amazon are already running pilot programs with humanoid workers.

slides.heading: The Dexterity Problem
slides.body: Hands remain the hardest challenge. Picking up a grape without crushing it requires sensing, force control, and learned intuition that engineers are still working to crack at scale and speed.

slides.heading: Cost vs. Human Labor
slides.body: Current humanoid robots cost $20,000–$150,000 each. Analysts predict prices could drop below $10,000 by 2030 as production scales, potentially making them cheaper per task than minimum-wage workers in many markets.

slides.heading: Ethical & Social Questions
slides.body: Who owns the data these robots collect in our homes and workplaces? How do we retrain displaced workers? And who is liable when a robot causes an accident? Society is only beginning to debate the answers.

slides.heading: A New Kind of Colleague
slides.body: Humanoid robots won't replace humanity — but they will redefine what work means. The decisions we make now about safety, fairness, and oversight will shape whether this revolution benefits everyone.
